chemistry.scope_summary: Study focused on photohydrolysis of mono-chloroacetic acid using UV radiation (λ < 270 nm) in microwave photoreactors with mercury electrodeless discharge lamps. Temperature effects on quantum yield and lamp intensity were investigated.

optimization_table.key_findings: Low-pressure batch microwave photoreactor at 80°C gave highest conversion (46%)

figures.flow_path: top inlet through condenser J → reactor A → bottom outlet through G → collection flask F → collection system B
figures.key_information: Continuous flow reactor setup with condenser, heated reactor vessel with mechanical stirring, and collection system with pump control
figures.caption: Figure 2.  Schematic diagram of the continuous-flow microwave photoreactor. (A) modified microwave oven with magnetron; (B) glass reservoir with magnetic stir bar; (C) thermometer; (D) pH-meter with a glass electrode; (E) magnetic stirrer; (F) PTFE diaphragm pump; (G) outlet; (H) spectrometer with a fiber-optic probe; (I) quartz tube with the quartz Hg-EDLs; and (J) cooling condenser.

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Low conversion (4%) at room temperature in classical immersion-well photoreactor
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Microwave heating allows higher temperatures (80°C) improving quantum yield
translation_logic.what_changed: Reactor type changed from classical immersion-well to microwave photoreactor, temperature increased from 20°C to 80°C
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Concentration (0.1 M), reaction time (120 min), UV light source type
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Microwave heating provides better temperature control

figure_descriptions: Continuous-flow microwave photoreactor schematic with components labeled

conflicts: Pass 1 claims this is 'both' batch and flow, but Pass 3 describes primarily batch microwave reactors with flow capability
notes: High-quality extraction with clear reactor scheme and optimization data. Some flow parameters are missing but batch baseline and optimized conditions are well documented. The study focuses on microwave-assisted photohydrolysis with detailed temperature optimization.
